chapters.title: An image is a record of how X sees Y
chapters.text:
Art history is a history of the visible. Early paintings were made to conserve time and conjure up the appearance of something absent. But the greatest works of art took a prominent place in cultural heritage because the creators have a unique way of seeing things.

The more imaginative the work, the more profoundly it allows us to share the artist’s experience of the visible. ~ John Berger

The artistic way of seeing is not just a personal skill. It is influenced by time and culture, religion, the aim of the painting, and the technologies used. Classical Renaissance art had a strict convention of perspective — everything was concentrated in the eye of the beholder. This idea was closely connected to Christianity because this particular eye was the eye of God, who created the surrounding world and imposed order upon it. After the world became less controlled by religion, it was no longer possible to see it this way. The invention of the camera showed that there is no center. Art had immediately reflected it in the avant-garde movements like impressionism, cubism, and expressionism.

Art is a mirror of the time in which we can see how an artist once looked at the surrounding world.

The focus of this summary is on four main ideas of the book:

• The reproductions of paintings have destroyed the uniqueness of their image.
• The European oil painting tradition shows two different types of presenting female characters wearing no clothes: nude and naked. Linguistically, these two mean the same thing, but there is a huge conceptual difference between those words, according to the author.
• Oil painting as a category of art is highly dependent on its owner’s interests: it should demonstrate the power of money.
• Nowadays, the role of oil paintings has passed to images, which we can see in public spaces. So-called “public images” are proposing to viewers a new way of seeing themselves after purchasing the advertised product.

chapters.title: Reproduction of art: how it changed the meaning of original work
chapters.text:
Before the 19 century, a work of art was always connected to the place where it resided. For example, the Dutch stills of the 1600s were usually hanging on the wall in rooms meant for meetings. These paintings performed the social function of conversation starters. However, if we see them now in a museum or online, we will perceive them in an entirely different way.

When paintings were created as ordered, they were destined for specific places. The place determined the meaning of the painting.

Since a camera can reproduce a painting, that work of art can have unlimited meanings because it may be a part of an unlimited number of situations. For example, if you put a postcard with the Mona Lisa by Leonardo da Vinci on your mood board — it would represent a good memory of Paris. In this sense, all paintings are contemporary if they still appear in our lives — on TV, on t-shirts, and on postcards. Owing to these advances, the greatest paintings have become ephemeral, ubiquitous, and available for everyone for the first time.

Instead, the original work of art had only one meaning (a lady's portrait) before being reproduced. The value of the original work of art is no longer in what it means but in what it is. The value depends upon its rarity. And also the price reflects it.

We often don't feel excited when we see an original work of art because we saw its reproduction on a smartphone. We treat the original work as holy relics: proof that the masterpiece exists. And the museums and galleries are producing a lot of texts to prove the value of the paintings they own. For example, in a catalog of the National Gallery, we can read about the Virgin of the Rocks painting by Leonardo da Vinci. The text tells things like who commissioned the painting, who owned it, and the likely date of its creation. All this information does not reveal the meaning of the painting but proves that you are seeing the unique original work of art.

chapters.title: Reproduced painting is always defined by the context
chapters.text:
When you see a work of art in a museum, it is surrounded by other self-sufficient works of art. But when you see its reproduction, it is usually surrounded by text or other pictures, as mentioned at the end of the previous chapter. This context influences the meaning of the picture and makes it more particular, complex, and deep.

Imagine viewing a landscape painted by Van Gogh in a museum. It depicts a cornfield with birds flying out of it. You can like it or not, think about your associations, or pass it by.

But when you see the reproduction of the painting in a book, you perceive the image along with the text — “this is the lost picture that Van Gogh painted before he killed himself.” From the moment you read the sentence, you could not analyze the image separated from the context in which it had been created. The painting now illustrates the context.

The meaning of an image is changed according to what one sees immediately beside it or what comes immediately after it. ~ John Berger

Since the invention of reproductions, paintings have lost their previous status. They are not magical or sacred anymore because anyone can use them as they please and surround them with any words and meanings. For example, we can find reproductions that single out a single detail. That detail could then be used beyond the context of the whole image and thus acquire a controversial meaning. If you see the cropped image of a beautiful woman, you may never know that she is an allegorical personification of justice in the whole painting.

Thanks to reproductions, any work of art can be contemporary. Therefore, its story continues if we use it in the actual context.

In this case, reproduced images could become political, commercial, personal, or social. Their existence might illustrate someone's ideas or theories. Even the book this summary is based on can be an example; it contains a lot of reproductions of paintings, but without the text, they have nothing in common. The context unites them and gives them additional meaning.

Did you know? The Mona Lisa by Leonardo da Vinci became an incredibly famous painting thanks to reproductions. When stolen in 1911, newspapers all over the world ran headlines about the missing masterpiece.

chapters.title: Naked and nude woman in European oil painting
chapters.text:
The first nudity in European painting is a traditional plot depicting Adam and Eve. They weren't aware of being naked before they ate the apple. At that moment, each saw the other differently. To wit, nakedness was created in the beholder's mind by the act of seeing.

This idea remained the same until the 20th century; the painting with a naked woman is a painting of a woman aware of being seen nude. And this is the difference between naked and nude. Berger puts it as such: to be naked is to be oneself, but to be nude means to be seen naked by the spectator. This idea soon became popular among feminists, including the British film critic Laura Mulvey. She used Berger’s theory to critique how traditional media represents female characters in cinema.

Up to the 20th century, almost all European oil paintings of female nudity were made by men and for men.

By the way, this specific view of female nudity is primarily standard for European tradition. Instead, non-European traditions of painting include lots of images of couples making love, where the woman is not posing for the spectator but has agency alongside her partner in the plot.

If we see lovers in European oil paintings, we can admit that a woman’s attention is not directed toward her partner. Often, she looks away and pretends to be uninterested in the contact. This type of women’s representation is still used in advertising and cinema, and we will discuss the woman’s unfocused look more in the last part of this summary.

Since the Renaissance, most European sexual imagery presents a frontal view, whether literally or metaphorically, because the viewer, acting as the spectator-owner, is positioned as the central figure.The exceptions are the paintings of loved women and wives of the artists. In this case, we will see the nude woman by herself and the relationship between the model and the creator of her image. The woman's will to be seen nude, her intentions, and her emotions are brought to our attention. We can notice their relationship and recognize ourselves as outsiders. This type of painting is the only case when nakedness is non-anonymous.

Did you know? The National Gallery (London), spanning over 2,300 paintings from the 13th to the early 20th century, has only 21 paintings created by female artists.

chapters.title: Buying a painting also means buying the look of the thing it represents
chapters.text:
The traditional European oil painting period was between the 1500s and 1900s. It was highly dependent on its owner’s interests as an art form because buying art was a privilege of the wealthy minority. Indeed, an oil painting was a celebration of private property.

Art collectors invested money in the culture not just because of their love of art but also because of the desire to have unique objects. For example, among the paintings depicting interiors, there existed a particular genre showing private artwork collections and their owners till the 20th century. Therefore, the customer wanted to have a painting that captured the dimensions of their artwork collection at a specific time. In this way, the oil painting was supposed to show the power of the owner’s assets.

The oil painting in its traditional period was a visualization of what could be bought or possessed by a wealthy man.

After all, oil painting was not a demonstration of the subject and its qualities but the value it gave to its owner:

• The family portrait is a demonstration of lineage.
• Paintings of animals are proof of their value and the social status of their owners.
• Paintings of buildings are a demonstration of landed property.

Even the highest category in oil painting — the mythological picture — demonstrated a particular moral value. If you own such a painting, you have an example of how the heightened moments of life (power, love, passion, or death) should be lived. Or at least should be seen to be lived.

All this became possible because of a particular characteristic of oil paint. It could render the texture, the tangibility, and the solidity of what it depicts. The final work of art could be so good that the viewer can easily imagine the subject for real.

chapters.title: Publicity images as a resumption of traditional oil painting ideas
chapters.text:
The author uses the term “publicity images” as a common name for all images placed in public spaces, such as images on advertising billboards, banners, signs, and in magazines, newspapers, promotional materials, and booklets.

We see different publicity images all the time. Perhaps even now, when you are reading or listening to this article, you are surrounded by images. For the first time in humankind's history, we can see such a concentration of images and an accumulation of visual messages. Interestingly, we are used to such an abundance of visual content, and we can understand it even without focusing our attention on it.

We accept the system of publicity images as a regular part of the world, as a phenomenon of nature.

Publicity images promise the transformation of our lives and ourselves if we buy what is advertised through them. What message do such images carry for us? The image offers us a new look of ourselves made more glamorous by the product we buy. It steals our love for ourselves and offers it back for the price of the new product.

How are those images doing it? By showing us people who have already been transformed and now are better than we are. Remember at least one advertisement for a fitness club or cosmetics to understand the idea behind a publicity image.

Unlike oil painting, publicity images are never about the happiness of possession but an opportunity to become different.

Anonymity is one of the instruments publicity images use to impact us. The more impersonal the image is, the greater the illusion of its power. It is the same as discussing nudity in the oil painting tradition. The nude woman usually has an absent, unfocused look because seeing is an act of choice. To see means to be in a powerful position. The woman is not looking at the spectator so that he can look at her and place himself in the imagined situation.

Publicity images also use quotes from traditional oil paintings to increase the value of their message. The gestures, the romantic use of nature, the poses, and the materials symbolizing luxury are all the same! A knight becomes a motorist, and The Madonna becomes a caring mother choosing the best baby food.

In the previous chapter, we talked about oil paint as a perfect material for depicting other objects. From the 20th century on, cheap color photography took over this role. It can reproduce objects' colors and textures with even greater similarity and is one of the reasons why contemporary artists do not try to copy the surrounding world. They don’t use paint to imitate other objects. They use it as a material that can help to create something new.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
The act of looking has formed the visual culture. Every image (painted or made with a camera) is an author's way of seeing the subject. But in the moment of artwork creation, the story of the gaze does not end. Every time we look at a work of art, we do it in our context. Therefore, our way of looking can change the meaning of the painting. Furthermore, our form of treating the reproduction of the painting can do the same.

In this summary, you may have learned a lot about European oil paintings. So it would be beneficial to get familiar with some examples that gave impetus to the authors’ analyses. You can look up these reproductions to get a complete picture:

• The Venus of Urbino by Titian

This painting is a good illustration of Berger's conception of nudity. We see Venus posing in front of the viewer and touching herself. The image was definitely made to provoke erotic thoughts and associations.

• Hen Pelsken by Peter Paul Rubens

In Chapter 4, we talked about paintings of artists’ loved women and wives. In this case, nudity is not an invitation to the beholder to participate in the scene. On the contrary, it is a particular moment of real relations the artist captured.

• The Ambassadors by Hans Holbein the Younger

This painting is an addition to the fifth chapter of the summary, which shows how status objects are essential to be seen. The painting’s detail is so high that we can easily imagine how these objects looked five centuries ago.

• Picture gallery of carinal Valenti Gonzaga by Panini

This work is an example of paintings showing a great private art collection. It was made as proof of the high social status of its owner. We use our Instagram pages nowadays for the same reasons!

It may sound trite to say that art is human-made, but it is not just something created by a human; art captures the unique gaze of the beholder and the reality it channels through. Humans of the 1700s, for example, had a lot in common with us; remember that while meeting with works of art! They are not merely containers of history or symbolism; they are a human’s sight captured with oil paint.

Try this

1. Think if you have some reproduction of artwork that has a special meaning to you. And then search for the original purpose of the work.
2. Have a look at the four paintings described in the conclusion.
3. Compare Beyoncé's music video for the song Mine and the Pieta by Michelangelo. Could you notice how similar they are? Why do you think it is like that?
